notes: Chef review wave 12. Backstage source notes only; public pages should remain recipe-first.

recipes.review_summary: The Sahrawi camel stew was plausible but needed garlic, longer cooking, and no halal guarantee. This pass keeps the desert camel, onion, tomato, and communal bread context.
recommended_changes: Cook camel low and slow until tender.
Add garlic alongside onion and tomato.
Serve with flatbread and tea.
recipes.official_source_status: Direct Sahrawi camel stew recipes are scarce. Western Saharan food references consistently identify camel as a favored meat and mention meat stews with onion, garlic, and bread.

recipe.description: Sahrawi-style camel meat stew with onions, garlic, tomatoes, potatoes, cumin, coriander, and slow desert cooking.
recipe.story: Camel needs patience. Brown it first, then let the pot simmer until the meat relaxes into the tomato-onion sauce.
recipe.best_served_with: Flatbread, couscous, dates, or Sahrawi mint tea

recipes.review_summary: Sahrawi mint tea was accurate. This pass removes halal as irrelevant and keeps the three-round foam ritual.
recommended_changes: Use gunpowder green tea, sugar, and mint.
Pour from height to build foam.
Serve in three rounds, getting milder and sweeter.
recipes.official_source_status: Sahrawi and Maghrebi tea references consistently describe green tea with mint and sugar, poured from height and served in repeated rounds.

sources.title: How to Brew Sahrawi Tea the Traditional Way

recipe.description: Sahrawi mint tea, strong gunpowder green tea with fresh mint and sugar, poured from height and served in three rounds.
recipe.story: Foam matters. Pour high, return the tea to the pot, and repeat until the glasses carry a pale frothy crown.
recipe.best_served_with: Dates, nuts, flatbread, camel stew, or couscous

recipes.review_summary: The couscous was plausible and well structured. This pass removes halal as irrelevant and adds chickpeas or raisins as optional desert pantry notes.
recommended_changes: Steam couscous in stages.
Use onions, carrots, turnips, squash, and light broth.
Add chickpeas or raisins if available.
recipes.official_source_status: Western Saharan references identify couscous as a staple served with meat and vegetables; precise household vegetable combinations vary.

recipe.description: Sahrawi vegetable couscous steamed with onion, carrot, turnip, squash, chickpeas, olive oil, and light broth.
recipe.story: The couscous should be steamed and fluffed, not soaked into paste. Treat the grains gently and let the vegetables season the broth.
recipe.best_served_with: Camel stew, goat, leben, harissa, or mint tea

recipes.review_summary: Chikanda was accurate but the timer ignored the chilling stage and halal was irrelevant. This pass makes the set time visible.
recommended_changes: Use chikanda orchid powder, ground peanuts, baking soda, chile, and salt.
Cook while stirring until thick.
Chill until firm before slicing.
recipes.official_source_status: Zambian references consistently describe chikanda as a savory cake made from wild orchid tubers and peanuts, cooked, set, and sliced.

recipe.description: Zambian chikanda, African polony made from wild orchid tuber powder, ground peanuts, baking soda, chile, and salt.
recipe.story: Chikanda has to set before it slices cleanly. Cook it thick, press it firmly, then give it time to chill.
recipe.best_served_with: Nshima, tea, tomato relish, chile sauce, or salad

recipes.review_summary: Kapenta was accurate. This pass removes halal and keeps the rinse, fry, tomato-onion relish, and nshima pairing.
recommended_changes: Rinse dried kapenta well.
Fry before adding tomatoes and onions.
Serve with nshima.
recipes.official_source_status: Zambian references consistently describe kapenta as small dried fish fried with tomato and onion and eaten with nshima.

recipe.description: Zambian kapenta, tiny dried fish rinsed, fried crisp, then cooked with onion, tomato, garlic, green pepper, and chile.
recipe.story: Fry the kapenta first so it stays savory and firm before the tomato relish wraps around it.
recipe.best_served_with: Nshima, rice, ifisashi, tomato relish, or greens

recipes.review_summary: Nshima with Ifisashi was accurate. This pass removes halal and adds more flexible greens.
recommended_changes: Use maize meal for nshima.
Cook greens in peanut sauce with tomato and onion.
Use rape, pumpkin leaves, cassava leaves, spinach, or other greens.
recipes.official_source_status: Zambian references consistently describe ifisashi as greens cooked in peanut sauce and served with nshima.

recipe.description: Zambian nshima with ifisashi, firm maize porridge served with leafy greens cooked in tomato-onion peanut sauce.
recipe.story: The ifisashi should be creamy but not heavy. Let the greens soften before the peanut sauce thickens around them.
recipe.best_served_with: Kapenta, beans, chicken, tomato relish, or chile

replace_ingredients.name: rape leaves, pumpkin leaves, spinach, or cassava leaves

recipes.review_summary: Dovi was sound but needed garlic, ginger, and pepper in the ingredient list. This pass keeps chicken, peanut butter, tomato, greens, and sadza service.
recommended_changes: Brown chicken first.
Cook onion, garlic, ginger, tomato, pepper, and peanut butter into a sauce.
Add greens near the end.
recipes.official_source_status: Zimbabwean dovi references consistently describe chicken or vegetables stewed with peanut butter, tomato, onion, garlic, pepper, and greens.

recipe.description: Zimbabwean dovi, chicken stewed with peanut butter, tomatoes, onion, garlic, ginger, pepper, and pumpkin leaves or spinach.
recipe.story: Dovi is gentle comfort, but the peanut must cook through. Simmer until the sauce is creamy and no longer raw-tasting.
recipe.best_served_with: Sadza, rice, muriwo, roasted vegetables, or fresh tomato salad

recipes.review_summary: Madora was plausible. This pass removes halal and keeps the soaking, frying, tomato-onion sauce, and optional peanuts.
recommended_changes: Soak dried mopane worms until flexible.
Fry to firm the texture.
Finish with onion, tomato, garlic, chile, and optional crushed peanuts.
recipes.official_source_status: Zimbabwean references consistently identify madora or mopane worms as a common protein eaten dried, fried, and often cooked with tomato and onion.

recipe.description: Zimbabwean madora, dried mopane worms soaked, fried crisp, and cooked with onion, tomato, garlic, chile, and optional peanuts.
recipe.story: The soak softens them; the fry gives them bite. Finish in tomato relish so the flavor sits comfortably with sadza.
recipe.best_served_with: Sadza, muriwo, tomato relish, roasted peanuts, or rice

recipes.review_summary: Mapopo candy was accurate but the timer ignored drying. This pass adds drying/resting time to the total.
recommended_changes: Use green papaya.
Cook slowly in syrup until translucent.
Dry the candy before storing.
recipes.official_source_status: Zimbabwe recipe references describe mapopo candy as green papaya cooked in sugar syrup, often with lemon, mint, vanilla, or color, then dried.

recipe.description: Zimbabwean mapopo candy, green papaya simmered in sugar syrup with lemon, then dried until jewel-like and lightly crystallized.
recipe.story: The syrup makes it translucent, but the drying makes it candy. Give the pieces time on a rack before storing.
recipe.best_served_with: Tea, coffee, fresh fruit, or as a small sweet after sadza

recipes.review_summary: Sadza neNyama was sound but the stew needs a little more time and no halal guarantee. This pass keeps sadza, beef, tomato-onion gravy, and greens.
recommended_changes: Cook beef stew until tender.
Make sadza from a thin starter porridge before thickening.
Serve with muriwo.
recipes.official_source_status: Zimbabwean references consistently describe sadza as a staple maize porridge served with nyama, muriwo, or other relishes.

recipe.description: Zimbabwean sadza ne nyama, thick maize sadza served with slow-cooked beef, tomato, onion, and garlic gravy.
recipe.story: Sadza is plain by design, so the nyama must carry the sauce. Cook the beef until tender before you start the maize pot.
recipe.best_served_with: Muriwo, covo, tomato relish, beans, or avocado

recipes.review_summary: Sadza with Muriwo was accurate but had an optional peanut butter ingredient while claiming nut-free. This pass removes peanut from this plain muriwo version.
recommended_changes: Use maize sadza with greens, onion, tomato, and garlic.
Keep peanut butter out of this nut-free version.
Serve as a simple everyday plate.
recipes.official_source_status: Zimbabwean references consistently describe sadza with muriwo as maize porridge served with leafy greens cooked with onion and tomato.

sources.title: I've Been Cooking - Sadza ne Muriwo

recipe.description: Zimbabwean sadza with muriwo, thick maize sadza served with leafy greens cooked in onion, tomato, garlic, and oil.
recipe.story: This is everyday comfort: firm sadza, soft greens, and enough tomato-onion sauce to make each pinch satisfying.
recipe.best_served_with: Nyama, beans, dovi, avocado, or tomato relish

replace_ingredients.name: covo, rape, collard greens, or spinach
